title: Will AI make companies outsource more, or less?
gist: Applying Coase/Williamson transaction-cost theory to AI, Smith argues AI could either drive a world of solopreneurs and total outsourcing or, by making counterparties cheaper to fake and harder to keep consistent, raise transaction costs and favor bigger firms, plausibly producing a bifurcated economy of many solopreneurs plus a few giant companies.

insights.claim: US new-business formation surged after the pandemic and showed no sign of reverting to pre-pandemic levels even in 2024, reversing a long pre-pandemic decline in business dynamism.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A durable upshift in business formation reshapes the SMB/startup landscape and the addressable market for tools serving small firms.
insights.quote: even in 2024 the trend in business applications showed no sign of reverting to its pre-pandemic level

insights.claim: Solopreneurship (businesses with no employees) has risen since 2008 in both absolute terms and as a share of new business formation, accelerated by the pandemic and aided by Obamacare exchanges, the Qualified Business Income deduction, and simplified home-office tax rules.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Identifies the policy and platform tailwinds behind solopreneurship, relevant to fintech, SaaS, and gig-economy platforms that monetize one-person businesses.
insights.quote: Solopreneurship has been increasing since 2008, both in absolute terms and as a percent of new business formation.
insights.locator: The upshot is that there's a big trend

insights.claim: Stripe Economics argues AI is the true engine of the recent surge in business formation, with nonemployer-business growth positively related to industry-level AI adoption, and treats AI's ~20% impact as a floor not a ceiling.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: If AI is driving solo-business creation, AI-tooling and payments platforms (Stripe, Substack-like) capture an expanding base of one-person firms.
insights.quote: We think this phenomenon is the true engine of the AI surge in business formation we're seeing today.
insights.locator: The team at Stripe Economics argues

insights.claim: Kim and Koning's working paper finds AI-native companies are being created with about 25% fewer employees than their peers.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence AI lowers headcount per firm, bearish for aggregate white-collar headcount growth and relevant to labor-demand forecasts.
insights.quote: "AI-native" companies now being created tend to have about 25% fewer employees than their peers
insights.locator: Kim and Koning have a new working paper

insights.claim: Transaction-cost theory (Coase, Williamson) holds that firms exist because some transactions are cheaper in-house than at arm's length, and the internet lowered those costs, driving the multi-decade wave of corporate outsourcing.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Establishes the analytical engine for predicting firm size: whatever moves transaction costs moves the in-house-vs-outsource boundary.
insights.quote: companies exist because it's sometimes cheaper and easier to execute transactions in-house than at arm's length
insights.locator: First advanced by Ronald Coase in the 1930s

insights.claim: Bergeaud et al. (2023) find the staggered diffusion of broadband internet in France drove firms to increase outsourcing while reducing the diversity of in-house occupations, empirically confirming technology-driven outsourcing.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Empirical anchor that falling transaction costs cause outsourcing, the base case AI bulls extrapolate from.
insights.quote: connected firms increased their outsourcing expenditures while decreasing the diversity of occupations they employ in-house

insights.claim: Contrary to the assumption that AI lowers transaction costs like the internet only more so, AI may raise them because AI does not just analyze information but creates it, letting bad actors fabricate fake companies, websites, and reviews to defraud counterparties.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A non-consensus thesis that AI could re-internalize work into firms, the opposite of the popular solopreneur-everywhere narrative.
insights.quote: it's possible to imagine that AI will increase transaction costs between companies. The simple reason is that AI doesn't just analyze information; it also creates it.
insights.locator: But at the same time, it's possible to imagine

insights.claim: AI-driven fraud is already happening at scale, with foreign firms using AI-generated images and false back stories to pose as family-run UK businesses, per a BBC report and Which?.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Real-world evidence that AI lowers the cost of fabricating trust, raising verification costs for procurement and e-commerce platforms.
insights.quote: the growing use of AI tools was making it possible for fraudsters to mislead the public on an "unprecedented" scale
insights.locator: Here's a BBC story from last year

insights.claim: AI agents are ephemeral and inconsistent over time, so trust between counterparties may have to be re-established from scratch periodically, making long-term arm's-length B2B relationships expensive to maintain.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Agent inconsistency is a hidden cost of agentic commerce; persistent agent identity/reliability becomes a valuable feature to build or buy.
insights.quote: their ability to maintain the same goals, personalities, and capabilities over the weeks or months or even years of a business-to-business contractor relationship has yet to be demonstrated
insights.locator: This is not generally true with AI

insights.claim: Bitcoin is a cautionary example of how expensive constant re-establishment of trust is: to avoid intermediaries it must re-verify trust every transaction, which (per Eric Budish) is so electricity-intensive it dooms Bitcoin as a medium of exchange.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Analogizes agentic-commerce trust costs to Bitcoin's proven-expensive trustless model, a bearish framing for trustless AI-to-AI transacting.
insights.quote: constant reestablishment of trust is incredibly expensive in terms of electricity, which basically dooms Bitcoin's use as a medium of exchange

insights.claim: If AI raises transaction costs, the AI age will favor bigger companies that can establish internal trust cheaply, and Japan-style salaryman jobs (shifting bundles of tasks within one big firm) may become more common worldwide.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A scenario where AI consolidates rather than fragments firms, implying scale advantages for incumbents and a shift toward generalist in-firm roles.
insights.quote: If only big companies can establish internal trust cheaply, then many humans...will be working for big companies.
insights.locator: If only big companies can establish internal trust

insights.claim: The likely outcome is a bifurcated distribution of company sizes: a vast horde of solopreneurs in lines of business where long-term inter-agent trust does not matter, plus a few monster companies employing huge numbers of wage earners where it does.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Predicts a barbell economy (micro-firms + mega-firms) hollowing out mid-size firms, a structural lens for equity and labor-market exposure.
insights.quote: a vast horde of solopreneurs, and a few monster companies employing huge numbers of wage earners
insights.locator: We may thus see a bifurcated distribution
